1	---
2	name: lean-vs-blueprint-checker
3	description: Per-file bidirectional verifier. Reads one .lean file and its blueprint chapter; reports (a) whether the Lean file follows the blueprint — fake/placeholder statements, signature mismatches with \lean{...} hints, proof divergence, "temporary" defs — AND (b) whether the blueprint chapter is detailed enough to have guided a faithful formalization of this file.

5	read_only: true
6	can_spawn: false
7	default_enabled: false
8	mandatory: [review]
9	dispatcher_notes: |
10	  - I am highly recommended in the review phase. Dispatch one checker
11	    per `.lean` file that received prover work this iteration. Each
12	    dispatch is independent and parallel-able.
13	
14	    **You may skip me this iter when NO `.lean` file received prover
15	    work** (e.g. the prover phase was skipped, or every prover
16	    INCOMPLETE'd without committing edits). Record under `## Subagent
17	    skips` in `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`:
18	    ``- lean-vs-blueprint-checker: no .lean files modified this iter
19	    (no prover edits to verify)``.
20	
21	    Do NOT skip a per-file dispatch when the prover DID commit edits
22	    to that file — skipping a prover-touched file is the review-phase
23	    failure this affordance exists to surface, not to enable. The
24	    "highly recommended" framing applies to the whole-iter decision,
25	    not to picking-and-choosing within an iter that has multiple
26	    prover-touched files.
27	  - Also dispatch on any file a code-audit subagent in your catalog

33	  - I report bidirectionally: Lean → blueprint AND blueprint → Lean.
34	    The blueprint can be the failure (too thin to guide
35	    formalization, missing the level of detail the Lean code clearly
36	    needed), not only the Lean. If I flag a chapter as inadequate,

38	    subagent to address it.
39	  - Any must-fix-this-iter finding I report blocks downstream work on
40	    the affected file/chapter until addressed. Soft enforcement: the
41	    plan agent reads my report and follows the gate rules in plan.md.
42	---
43	
44	# Lean ↔ Blueprint Checker
45	
46	You compare **one Lean file** against **its blueprint chapter** and report whether the Lean follows the blueprint faithfully. You are read-only.
47	
48	## Your Job
49	
50	Your directive names one `.lean` file (e.g. `Algebra/WLocal.lean`) and its corresponding blueprint chapter (e.g. `blueprint/src/chapters/Algebra_WLocal.tex`). You audit **bidirectionally**:
51	
52	### (A) Does the Lean follow the blueprint?
53	
54	For each `\lean{...}`-tagged block in the chapter, verify the Lean side:
55	
56	- **Declaration exists** with the right name (matches `\lean{namespace.theorem_name}`).
57	- **Signature matches** the informal statement in the chapter — the types are what the prose says they should be. A statement that type-checks but uses the wrong type class (e.g. `Smooth f` where the prose says "smooth of relative dimension n") is **wrong**, not "approximate".
58	- **Proof follows the blueprint sketch** — the proof in the Lean file should be a formalization of the steps in the chapter's `\begin{proof}...\end{proof}`, not a different argument that happens to produce the same conclusion.
59	- **No fake / placeholder statements** — definitions or theorems whose body is just `:= sorry` or a trivially-true tautology while the chapter prose claims something substantive.
60	- **No "temporary" definitions left in place** — comments like `-- TODO: replace with the real definition`, `-- placeholder until we figure out X`, or definitions whose body is suspicious (e.g. `def Foo := True`, weakened-wrong definitions standing in for the real concept).
61	- **No comments that explain away wrong code** — "we are using a wrong definition for now", "this proof is incomplete but will be fixed", etc. These are red flags, not acceptable workflow notes.
62	
63	### (B) Is the blueprint adequate for this Lean file?
64	
65	The blueprint can also be the failure mode. For the chapter you're reading, check:
66	
67	- **Every Lean declaration in this file should map to a blueprint block** (via `\lean{...}`) OR be an explicitly-helper-only declaration. If the file has substantive declarations the blueprint doesn't reference at all, the chapter is incomplete.
68	- **Proof sketches in the blueprint should be detailed enough** for a prover to formalize correctly. If you see Lean proofs that clearly went through significant reasoning the blueprint's `\begin{proof}` does NOT preview, flag the chapter as under-specified.
69	- **`\lean{...}` hints should pin the Lean target precisely.** If the blueprint says "smooth morphism" without specifying which Mathlib predicate (`Smooth` vs `SmoothOfRelativeDimension n` vs `IsSmooth`), and the Lean file ended up with the wrong one, that's BOTH a Lean error AND a blueprint clarity failure. Flag both sides.
70	- **Definitions in the blueprint should be at the right level of generality** for the Lean to consume. If the blueprint defines something narrower than the project actually needs and the Lean had to write a parallel API to cover the gap, that's a blueprint adequacy failure.
71	
72	Both directions matter. Don't excuse a wrong Lean statement by saying "the blueprint is loose"; don't excuse a thin blueprint by saying "the Lean works anyway." Report both.
73	
74	## Directive Format
75	
76	```markdown
77	# Lean ↔ Blueprint Checker Directive
78	
79	## Slug
80	<slug>
81	
82	## Lean file
83	<path/to/file.lean>
84	
85	## Blueprint chapter
86	blueprint/src/chapters/<slug>.tex
87	
88	## Known issues
89	<things the review agent already knows and doesn't want re-reported>
90	```
91	
92	That's it. No strategy snapshot, no references, no full project context — just the two files and any pre-known issues to skip. Cross-file consistency is the job of the code-audit and blueprint-review subagents in the catalog; you stay focused on this one pair.
93	
94	## What you do
95	
96	1. Read your directive.
97	2. Read the Lean file completely. Note every declaration (`def`, `theorem`, `lemma`, `instance`, `axiom`, …) and its signature.
98	3. Read the blueprint chapter completely. List every `\lean{...}` reference and its corresponding declaration block.
99	4. **For each `\lean{...}` block**: locate the Lean declaration. Verify:
100	   - It exists.
101	   - Its signature matches the informal statement (use `archon-lean-lsp` for hover/signature info).
102	   - If the Lean has a body (proof or definition body), does it match the blueprint's prose? Naming and tactic choice can differ; the *mathematical content* should match.
103	5. **For each declaration in the Lean file**: is it `\lean{...}`-referenced from the blueprint? Unreferenced declarations are notable but not necessarily wrong (helpers may exist). Flag suspect ones.
104	6. **Scan for red flags** in the Lean file:
105	   - `:= sorry` where the blueprint claims a substantive statement (placeholder).
106	   - Suspect bodies like `:= True`, `:= rfl` for non-trivial claims, `:= Classical.choice _`.
107	   - Comments excusing wrong/incomplete code: `-- TODO replace with real def`, `-- temporary`, `-- will fix later`, `-- this is wrong but works for now`.
108	   - `axiom` declarations.
109	7. **Cross-check** any `import` paths and namespace usage that should match what the blueprint's `\lean{...}` hints suggest.
110	
111	You may use:
112	- `archon-lean-lsp` MCP tools (read-only): hover, signature lookup, diagnostics.
113	- `sorry_analyzer` if available, to count sorries in the file.
114	
115	You may NOT modify the Lean file, the blueprint, or any other project file. Even when a fix is obvious.
116	
117	## Report format
118	
119	Write your report to `.archon/task_results/lean-vs-blueprint-checker-<slug>.md` (or the parent-aware path when invoked nested — your invocation prompt names the exact path).
120	
121	```markdown
122	# Lean ↔ Blueprint Check Report
123	
124	## Slug
125	<slug>
126	
127	## Iteration
128	<NNN>
129	
130	## Files audited
131	- Lean: <path>
132	- Blueprint: <path>
133	
134	## Per-declaration
135	
136	For every `\lean{...}` block in the chapter, one entry:
137	
138	### `\lean{Foo.bar}` (chapter: \thm:bar)
139	- **Lean target exists**: yes / no (if no: declaration name in the .lean that comes closest, or "none found")
140	- **Signature matches**: yes / partial / no (with one line on the mismatch)
141	- **Proof follows sketch**: yes / partial / no / N/A (if N/A: chapter doesn't have a proof body to compare against)
142	- **notes**: <free-form, one line per observation>
143	
144	### `\lean{Foo.baz}` ...
145	
146	## Red flags
147	
148	(Section appears only if there are findings.)
149	
150	### Placeholder / suspect bodies
151	- `Foo.bar` at line 42: body is `:= sorry` but blueprint claims a substantive proof.
152	- `Foo.qux` at line 78: defined as `:= True`, suspect.
153	- ...
154	
155	### Excuse-comments
156	- `Foo.lean:113`: comment "TODO: replace with real def" attached to `Foo.frob`. Blueprint claims `Foo.frob` is the actual definition, not a placeholder.
157	- ...
158	
159	### Axioms / Classical.choice on non-trivial claims
160	- `Foo.lean:200`: `axiom` declaration `Foo.skip_step` introduced. Blueprint does not authorize this.
161	
162	## Unreferenced declarations (informational)
163	
164	<list of declarations in the Lean file that no `\lean{...}` references. Most are helpers — only flag the ones whose name suggests they should be in the blueprint.>
165	
166	## Blueprint adequacy for this file
167	
168	A bidirectional check: does the blueprint chapter give a prover enough detail to formalize this file correctly?
169	
170	- **Coverage**: <N>/<M> Lean declarations have a corresponding `\lean{...}` block in the chapter. Unreferenced declarations: <count> helpers (acceptable) + <count> substantive (flagged below).
171	- **Proof-sketch depth**: <one of: adequate / under-specified / silent>. If under-specified or silent, list which `\thm:...` blocks need expansion.
172	- **Hint precision**: <one of: precise / loose / wrong>. Loose hints (the prose pins one Mathlib predicate but the `\lean{...}` doesn't, leaving the prover to guess) get flagged. Wrong hints (the `\lean{...}` names a declaration whose signature doesn't match the prose) are critical.
173	- **Generality**: <one of: matches need / too narrow / too broad>. If too narrow, name the parallel API that the project ended up writing because the blueprint didn't cover the needed level of generality.
174	- **Recommended chapter-side actions**: <bullet list of items a blueprint-writing subagent should land if you flagged anything above>
175	
176	## Severity summary
177	
178	Apply these rules verbatim. Do NOT under-classify to soften the blow — the plan agent's gates depend on accurate severity, and a hidden critical finding hardens into shipped wrong code.
179	
180	- **must-fix-this-iter** — every one of the following lands here, no exceptions:
181	  - Placeholder body (`:= sorry`, `:= True`, `:= rfl` on a non-trivial claim, suspect `:= Classical.choice _` patterns) on a declaration the blueprint claims is substantive.
182	  - Signature mismatch with the blueprint's prose — including using the wrong Mathlib predicate (e.g. `Smooth` vs `SmoothOfRelativeDimension n`) when the prose pins one specific predicate.
183	  - Excuse-comments (`-- TODO replace with real def`, `-- temporary`, `-- placeholder`, `-- wrong but works for now`) on declarations the blueprint claims are real.
184	  - Axioms or `Classical.choice _` on substantive claims the blueprint does NOT authorize.
185	  - Blueprint adequacy failure: chapter is so under-specified that a prover could not have formalized this file correctly from prose alone.
186	  - Weakened-wrong definitions: Lean defines a structurally-different stand-in (e.g. `Scheme.LineBundle := CommRing.Pic Γ(X,⊤)` when the blueprint defines it as an invertible quasi-coherent O_X-module).
187	- **major** — partial signature mismatches that are fixable in-place, missing `\lean{...}` references to declarations that the blueprint should reference, comments that are stale but not actively misleading.
188	- **minor** — naming drift, helpers worth promoting to blueprint blocks, low-impact prose-vs-Lean differences.
189	
190	Overall verdict: one sentence.
191	```
192	
193	The plan agent's per-file gate (see plan.md and the blueprint-review subagent's `dispatcher_notes` in the catalog) treats every must-fix-this-iter finding as blocking. If you classify a wrong signature as "major" to avoid the gate firing, you are working against the project, not for it.
194	
195	## Return value
196	
197	Your final assistant message:
198	
199	- One line: `<slug>: <overall verdict> — <N> declarations checked, <M> red flags`
200	- The path to your full report.
201	
202	## Reminders
203	
204	- **One file pair, no global context.** Cross-file consistency belongs to the catalog's code-audit and blueprint-review subagents.
205	- **You are read-only.** No project source, no blueprint, no state files (except your own report).
206	- **Excuse-comments are red flags, not workflow.** Treat "we use a wrong def for now" with the suspicion it deserves.
207	- **Mathematical content match, not syntactic.** The Lean proof can choose different tactics than the chapter's sketch implies, as long as the mathematical steps are the same.
208

1	# Archon Project
2	
3	You are one of: the plan agent, a prover agent, a subagent (per descriptor in `.archon/subagents/`), or the review agent. Read `PROGRESS.md` to determine your role and current objectives. Keep workspace tidy. Prefer existing MCP tools.
4	
5	## Priority Rule
6	
7	When instructions conflict between global and local sources, **local takes precedence**:
8	
9	- Prompts in `.archon/prompts/` override Archon's global prompts.
10	- Skills in `.claude/skills/` override globally installed plugins.
11	- Rules in `.claude/rules/` apply only to this project.
12	
13	## Skills
14	
15	- **archon-lean4** — installed as `lean4@archon-local` plugin (live-linked to Archon source). Provides `/archon-lean4:prove`, `/archon-lean4:golf`, `/archon-lean4:doctor`, etc.
16	
17	## Tools
18	
19	Project tools live in `.claude/tools/` as directly-executable scripts. List with `ls .claude/tools/`; run `<path> --help`. MCP servers (Lean LSP, etc.) are registered in `.claude/settings.json` / `.mcp.json` and surface as `mcp__*` tools.
20	
21	Two always-present scripts:
22	
23	- **`archon-informal-agent.py`** — call external LLMs for an informal proof-style sketch when you're stuck on an approach and want a second opinion. Supports DeepSeek, Kimi (OpenAI-compatible via `--provider kimi`), Kimi (Anthropic-compatible via `--provider kimi-anthropic`), OpenRouter, OpenAI, and Gemini; defaults to `--provider auto` which picks the best available key automatically. **Requires at least one key in env** (`DEEPSEEK_API_KEY` / `MOONSHOT_API_KEY` / `OPENROUTER_API_KEY` / `OPENAI_API_KEY` / `GEMINI_API_KEY`) — check via `env | grep -E "DEEPSEEK|MOONSHOT|OPENROUTER|OPENAI|GEMINI"` BEFORE planning around its use; if no key is set, fall back to alternatives below. Both `kimi` and `kimi-anthropic` use the same `MOONSHOT_API_KEY`; the latter speaks Moonshot's Anthropic-compatible Messages endpoint and supports `--think`. The output is LLM-generated from training data, NOT source-derived: this tool is **not** a literature retriever. For actual literature lookup (cross-check a claim against Hartshorne, fetch an arXiv paper, summarize a Stacks tag) consult the auto-injected subagent catalog — when a literature/reference-fetching subagent is enabled it will appear there — or use the `WebSearch` / `WebFetch` tools directly.
24	- **`archon-subagent.py`** — the generic subagent dispatcher (one wrapper handles every subagent — see "Subagents" below).
25	
26	## Subagents
27	
28	Subagents are descriptor files at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md` (YAML frontmatter — `name`, `description`, `write_domain`, `read_only`, `can_spawn`, `default_enabled`, optional `mandatory: [<phase>...]`, optional `dispatcher_notes` — followed by the prompt body the spawned Claude reads).
29	
30	**You do NOT need to discover subagents.** The plan and review prompts auto-inject an **Available subagents** section at the top of each invocation listing every enabled descriptor with description, write-domain, MANDATORY flag, and `dispatcher_notes`. When you decide to invoke a subagent, read its full prompt at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md`.
31	
32	**Invoke** via the generic wrapper (Bash tool, foreground):
33	
34	```
35	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
36	  --name <subagent-name> \
37	  --slug <kebab-slug> \
38	  --directive-file <path-to-directive.md> \
39	  --write-domain '<glob>'        # repeat for multiple
40	```
41	
42	The wrapper exits 0 on success, prints a one-line status, writes the report to `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`, and the Archon CLI auto-archives a copy to `logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-report.md` for the dashboard. The dispatch semaphore caps concurrent subagent processes at `loop.max_parallel`; the wrapper resolves `--parent-slug` from `ARCHON_SUBAGENT_SLUG` so nested children inherit the hierarchy.
43	
44	**Treat each dispatch as blocking — wait for its report before acting on it.** Don't *deliberately* set `run_in_background: true`. But note the wrapper is genuinely synchronous (it `subprocess.run`s the `archon subagent` CLI and only returns once the child finishes and its report is on disk), so the harness may *auto-background* a long-running dispatch and hand you a task ID immediately — that's expected and fine. When it does, await that task / poll for the report file at `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md` (the wrapper's one-line status and exit 0 confirm completion) before you read or act on the result. The thing to avoid is firing a dispatch and then continuing to plan as if it had already returned; whether the call blocked inline or was auto-backgrounded, the rule is the same: do not consume a subagent's output until its report exists.
45	
46	**Highly-recommended subagents.** A descriptor whose frontmatter sets `mandatory: [plan]` is treated as **highly recommended** for the plan phase (similarly `[review]` for the review phase). The catalog tags them `[HIGHLY RECOMMENDED]`. Dispatch each one unless you have a concrete reason to skip — when skipping, record a one-line rationale under a `## Subagent skips` section in `iter/iter-NNN/<phase>.md` (e.g. `- strategy-critic: STRATEGY.md SHA unchanged from prior iter and last verdict was SOUND`). A post-phase audit checks both signals: it silences when the subagent dispatched OR a skip rationale was recorded, and warns only when neither happened. Each subagent's `dispatcher_notes` enumerates its specific skip conditions — read them before deciding. **Subagents ship disabled by default** (`default_enabled: false`), so the rule only fires for subagents the user has explicitly listed under `subagents.enabled` in `.archon/config.json`; on a fresh project the catalog is empty and no recommended dispatch is required. (The frontmatter field is still named `mandatory: [...]` for backward compat with existing configs and descriptors — its semantic meaning is "highly recommended", not "must dispatch".)
47	
48	## Key Files & Permissions
49	
50	All Archon state files are in `.archon/`. The table below covers **phase agents** (plan, prover, review) and the **user**. Subagent permissions are NOT a fixed row — they are descriptor-driven (`write_domain` glob in the subagent's frontmatter, enforced at dispatch time). See "Subagent permissions" after the table.
51	
52	| File | Plan | Prover | Review | User |
53	|------|------|--------|--------|------|
54	| `PROGRESS.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
55	| `STRATEGY.md` | read + write | — | — | read |
56	| `USER_HINTS.md` | **loop-managed** (captured + cleared by the CLI; injected into the plan prompt) | — | — | write |
57	| `task_pending.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
58	| `task_done.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
59	| `task_results/<file>.md` | read (collect) | write (own file) | read only | read |
60	| `proof-journal/` | read | — | **write** | read |
61	| `PROJECT_STATUS.md` | read | — | **write** (Knowledge Base only — session log lives in `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`) | read |
62	| `iter/iter-NNN/plan.md` | **write** (this iter only) | — | read (recent window injected) | read |
63	| `iter/iter-NNN/review.md` | read (recent window injected) | — | **write** (this iter only) | read |
64	| `iter/iter-NNN/objectives.md` | optional write | — | read | read |
65	| `archon-protected.yaml` | read | read | read | **write** |
66	| `.lean` files | — | write (own file; frozen protected signatures) | — | write (via comments) |
67	| `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex` | **write** (informal prose, `\lean{...}` hints) | read | **write** (semantic markers: `\mathlibok`, `% NOTE:`, `\lean{...}` corrections — NOT `\leanok`) | read |
68	| `analogies/<slug>.md` | read (when relevant) | — | — | read |
69	| `TO_USER.md` | **write** (shared notice board) | **write** (shared notice board) | **write** (shared notice board) | read |
70	
71	### Subagent permissions
72	
73	Each subagent ships YAML frontmatter:
74	
75	- `write_domain: "<glob>"` — file pattern the subagent may write. Empty for purely read-only subagents (the dispatching call typically passes `--write-domain 'task_results/**'` so the subagent can still write its own report).
76	- `read_only: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may only write its `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`.
77	- `can_spawn: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may dispatch children whose `write_domain` is a subset of its own.
78	
79	The dispatcher enforces `write_domain` at the filesystem level: any child whose declared domain is not a subset of the parent's is rejected before the child agent starts. Reads are restricted only by the descriptor's prompt body (e.g. fresh-context critics that are told not to read PROGRESS.md / STRATEGY.md).
80	
81	**`.archon/REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md`** is reserved for the interactive `archon refactor draft` / `archon refactor run` flow run by hand by the mathematician. The autonomous loop never reads or writes that file. To invoke a refactor-style subagent inside the loop, write the directive to a fresh tempfile under `.archon/logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-directive.md` and dispatch via the generic wrapper. Older state files (`STRATEGY.md`, `task_pending.md`, `PROGRESS.md`) may contain leftover references to the old REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md flow — treat those as historical noise.
82	
83	## Protected Declarations
84	
85	`archon-protected.yaml` at the project root lists declarations whose **signatures are frozen** by the mathematician. Rules:
86	
87	- **Plan / prover / review** agents: read-only on protected signatures. May fill proof bodies; may not rename, re-type, or reorder arguments.
88	- **Write-capable structural subagents** (per their `write_domain`): may *move* a protected declaration to a different file (name + signature preserved verbatim) and must then update the path key in `archon-protected.yaml`. They may NEVER rename / re-type / delete / re-sign a protected declaration.
89	- The YAML file itself is user-only. No agent adds or removes a declaration. The only allowed agent edit is updating an existing declaration's file path.
90	
91	## Blueprint Marker Vocabulary
92	
93	Two semantic markers:
94	
95	- `\leanok` — statement block: declaration is formalized (at least a `sorry` present). Proof block: proof closed, no `sorry`.
96	- `\mathlibok` — statement block: declaration is backed by Mathlib (re-export / alias); no Archon proof obligation remains.
97	
98	No marker means the block is unformalized. If a block fails to translate, leave it unmarked and annotate with a `% NOTE:` comment.
99	
100	**`\leanok` is managed deterministically by the `sync_leanok` phase** that runs between prover and review each iteration. It walks every chapter, looks up each `\lean{...}` declaration, runs `sorry_analyzer` + `lake env lean`, and adds/removes `\leanok` accordingly. Agents must NOT add or remove `\leanok` themselves.
101	
102	`\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, and `% NOTE:` annotations are the review agent's domain (they require semantic judgement). The plan agent writes informal prose and `\lean{...}` hints; provers never touch the blueprint.
103	
104	## User Interaction
105	
106	The user provides hints in two places:
107	
108	- **Strategic hints** → `.archon/USER_HINTS.md`. The Archon loop captures this file's content before each plan phase, injects it into the plan agent's prompt, and resets the file to its bundled template (an HTML-comment format guide + zero bullets) after the plan phase succeeds. The HTML-comment preamble is stripped before injection so a template-only file renders as "no hints" to the planner. The plan agent does NOT read or clear that file itself. Provers never see these hints.
109	- **File-specific hints** → `/- USER: ... -/` comments directly in `.lean` files. The prover that owns that file sees them naturally.
110	
111	Archon talks back to the user through **`TO_USER.md`** — a single, *persistent* notice board surfaced as a UI banner. It is **shared and writable by every agent** (plan, prover, review). It is no longer cleared at the start of each review phase; content survives across iters until an agent prunes it. The discipline every writer follows:
112	
113	- **Concise**: the whole file stays ≤ 2–3 short bullets. It is a banner, not a log.
114	- **Relevant now**: before adding anything, read the current content and **delete any bullet that is no longer true at this point** (the blocker was resolved, the decision was overtaken, the credential got set). Never let it accumulate stale notices.
115	- **Notice board, never a question queue**: the loop is autonomous and may run unattended for many iters. Never write "awaiting your decision", an options menu, a "where to reply", or "default to X if no reply" — anything implying the loop is paused for a human. State decisions as *made* and blockers as *worked-around-as-far-as-possible*; the user steers asynchronously via `USER_HINTS.md`.
116	
117	## Agent Roles
118	
119	### Plan Agent
120	
121	- Read `.archon/prompts/plan.md` for full instructions.
122	- User hints, blueprint-doctor findings, recent iter sidecars, the subagent catalog, and the references summary are all pre-injected into your invocation prompt. You do not "go read" those files.
123	- Read `task_results/` to collect prover and subagent results, then update `task_pending.md` / `task_done.md`.
124	- Optionally invoke subagents (via the auto-injected catalog) before writing prover objectives. Subagent reports are auto-archived to `logs/iter-NNN/` by the Archon CLI.
125	- Write `PROGRESS.md` with objectives for the next prover round.
126	- Write informal prose in `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex`. Do NOT touch markers — `\leanok` is owned by the deterministic sync; `\mathlibok` / `% NOTE:` by the review agent.
127	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or fill sorries yourself.
128	
129	### Prover Agent
130	
131	- Read `PROGRESS.md` for your objectives (read only).
132	- Your **active mode** is injected directly into your invocation prompt — read it carefully, it defines your goal, workflow, and constraints for this session.
133	- When your mode specifies `read_blueprint: true`, you are permitted to **read** your assigned blueprint chapter (`blueprint/src/chapters/<your_slug>.tex`) for the informal proof sketch. You may never write to blueprint chapters.
134	- Write results to `task_results/<your_file>.md`.
135	- Write only to the `.lean` file(s) you are assigned — never edit another agent's file.
136	- Check for `/- USER: ... -/` comments in your file for file-specific hints.
137	- Do NOT edit blueprint chapters. Flag in your task result which declarations are ready for which marker; the review agent applies them.
138	
139	### Subagents
140	
141	Subagents are descriptor-driven and discovered at runtime. The catalog is auto-injected into the plan and review prompts each phase (see "Subagents" above for the wrapper invocation). Each subagent reads only what its directive points at — never `PROGRESS.md` / `STRATEGY.md` / phase-agent state — and writes a self-contained report at `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`. Descriptors with `can_spawn: true` may dispatch children (subject to the global `loop.max_parallel` cap).
142	
143	### Review Agent
144	
145	- Read `.archon/prompts/review.md` for full instructions.
146	- Read `proof-journal/current_session/attempts_raw.jsonl` for structured prover attempt data.
147	- Write the session journal to `proof-journal/sessions/session_N/` (`summary.md`, `milestones.jsonl`, `recommendations.md`).
148	- Update `PROJECT_STATUS.md` (Knowledge Base only — session narrative goes to `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`).
149	- Maintain the semantic blueprint markers — `\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, `% NOTE:` annotations, stale `\notready` removal. Do NOT touch `\leanok`.
150	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or modify `PROGRESS.md`.
151	
152	### Loop infrastructure (no agent role)
153	
154	These run automatically between phases:
155	
156	- **Iter sidecar init** — creates `.archon/iter/iter-NNN/` at iter start so plan + review have a stable destination for per-iter narrative. Top-level files (STRATEGY.md, PROJECT_STATUS.md, task_*.md) stay bounded.
157	- **`sync_leanok`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically updating `\leanok` markers based on actual sorry counts and compilation status.
158	- **`blueprint-doctor`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically detecting orphan chapters, broken `\ref` / `\uses` / `\proves`, and new `axiom` declarations. Findings are written to `logs/iter-NNN/blueprint-doctor.{md,json}` and inlined into the next plan agent's prompt.
159

10	# Generic flatness of a coherent sheaf (A.2.a)
11	
12	This file packages the classical Grothendieck/Nitsure~\S4 **generic flatness**
13	theorem: over a noetherian integral base `S`, a coherent `𝓞_X`-module on a
14	finite-type morphism `X ⟶ S` becomes flat after restricting to a non-empty open
15	subscheme of `S`. This is the inductive engine underlying the
16	flattening-stratification construction of Grothendieck's existence theorem for
17	the Quot scheme.
18	
19	Each blueprint-pinned declaration carries the *intended* substantive type
20	signature (matching the `\lean{...}` pin in
21	`blueprint/src/chapters/Picard_FlatteningStratification.tex`) with a `sorry`
22	body where the proof is not yet supplied. The substantive generic-flatness
23	proof is deep (Nitsure~\S4: prime-filtration dévissage + Noether normalisation +
24	clearing denominators).
25	
26	The blueprint-pinned declaration is:
27	
28	- `AlgebraicGeometry.genericFlatness` (theorem) — **generic flatness**
29	  [Nitsure §4 Theorem]: over a noetherian integral base `S`, a coherent sheaf
30	  on a finite-type `X ⟶ S` is flat over a non-empty open `V ⊆ S`. Its
31	  algebraic form (`thm:generic_flatness_algebraic`, no Lean pin) is the
32	  statement that a finite module over a finite-type algebra over a noetherian
33	  domain becomes free after inverting one non-zero element of the base.
34	
35	The file-internal `GenericFreeness` namespace lands the **finite-module /
36	finite-morphism special case** of the algebraic form axiom-clean, as a reusable
37	building block for the dévissage's leaves.
38	
39	## Note on type expressivity
40	
41	Following the project rule "Never weaken the type to dodge the proof",
42	`genericFlatness` requires the existence of a *non-empty* open `V` and
43	substantive flatness on every affine `U ⊆ V`; both quantifiers are necessary
44	(without them the statement collapses to `V = ∅`).
45	
46	## Mathlib status
47	
48	Mathlib (master `b80f227`) provides `Module.Flat`, `AlgebraicGeometry.Flat`
49	(morphism-level), `IsImmersion`, `IsLocallyNoetherian`, `IsIntegral`,
50	`LocallyOfFiniteType`, and the dévissage / Noether-normalisation infrastructure
51	the algebraic generic-flatness argument consumes. It does NOT yet provide the
52	polynomial-ring core of generic freeness, nor the geometric `genericFlatness`
53	statement packaging it over a noetherian integral base.
54	
55	## References
56	
57	Blueprint: `blueprint/src/chapters/Picard_FlatteningStratification.tex`.
58	Source: Nitsure, "Construction of Hilbert and Quot schemes", §4 (FGA Explained
59	Ch. 5, arXiv:math/0504020 pp. 5–18); Stacks Project tag 00HB (module flat).

71	
72	This section builds the **module-theoretic** generic-freeness statements that
73	underlie the geometric `genericFlatness` (blueprint
74	`thm:generic_flatness_algebraic`, Nitsure~\S4 "Lemma on Generic Flatness").
75	
76	The full algebraic statement — `A` a noetherian domain, `B` a *finite-type*
77	`A`-algebra, `M` a finite `B`-module ⟹ `∃ f ≠ 0` with `M_f` free over `A_f` —
78	is a deep theorem (prime-filtration dévissage + Noether normalisation +
79	clearing denominators). Mathlib already supplies most of the dévissage
80	machinery (`IsNoetherianRing.induction_on_isQuotientEquivQuotientPrime`,
81	`ModuleCat.free_shortExact`, `exists_finite_inj_algHom_of_fg`), but it does
82	**not** yet contain the polynomial-ring core (generic freeness for a finite
83	module over `A[X₁,…,X_d]`). See the file `task_results` handoff for the
84	precise decomposition of the remaining gap.
85	
86	What we *can* land axiom-clean here is the **finite-module / finite-morphism
87	case**: when `M` is finite as an `A`-module (in particular when `B` is
88	module-finite over `A`), generic freeness follows directly from
89	`Module.FinitePresentation.exists_free_localizedModule_powers` applied at the
90	generic point `Frac A`, where `M ⊗_A Frac A` is a finite vector space hence
91	free. This is a genuine special case of the algebraic generic-freeness
92	theorem (the case of a *finite* morphism `X → S`), and a reusable building
93	block for the dévissage's leaves. -/

1	\chapter{Flattening Stratification of a Coherent Sheaf}
2	\label{chap:Picard_FlatteningStratification}
3	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/FlatteningStratification.lean
4	
5	% STRATEGY NOTE. This chapter packages the classical Grothendieck/Nitsure~\S4
6	% \emph{generic flatness} theorem: over a noetherian integral base, a coherent
7	% sheaf on a finite-type \(X \to S\) becomes flat over a non-empty open
8	% subscheme of \(S\). Both its algebraic form (generic freeness of a finite
9	% module over a finite-type algebra over a noetherian domain) and its
10	% geometric form are recorded here as the prover-ready specification for
11	% \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/FlatteningStratification.lean}.
12	%
13	% This chapter is independently parallel-startable: it does not gate on the
14	% Route~A.1.a relative-Spec chapter \cref{chap:Picard_RelativeSpec}.
15	
16	\section{Setup and motivation}
17	\label{sec:flatstrat_setup}
18	
19	Generic flatness is the foundational input to the flattening-stratification
20	construction underlying Grothendieck's existence theorem for the Quot scheme
21	(\cite{nitsure-hilbert-quot}, \S4--\S5).  Its content is that flatness of a
22	coherent sheaf is a generic property over an integral base: over a noetherian
23	integral scheme \(S\), a coherent \(\OO_X\)-module on a finite-type morphism
24	\(X \to S\) becomes flat after restricting to a non-empty open subscheme of
25	\(S\). Algebraically this is the statement that a finite module over a
26	finite-type algebra over a noetherian domain becomes free after inverting a
27	single non-zero element of the base ring.
28	
29	We work throughout with a noetherian (integral, where required) base scheme
30	\(S\), a finite-type morphism \(p : X \to S\), and a coherent \(\OO_X\)-module
31	\(\F\).
32	
33	\section{Generic flatness}
34	\label{sec:generic_flatness}
35	
36	The construction of the flattening stratification proceeds by Noetherian
37	induction on \(S\). The induction step relies on the following classical
38	\emph{generic flatness} theorem of Grothendieck: over a noetherian integral
39	base, a coherent sheaf is flat over a non-empty open subscheme. Algebraically,
40	this is the assertion that a finitely generated module over a finite-type
41	algebra over a noetherian domain is generically free after inverting a single
42	non-zero element of the base ring.
43	
44	\begin{theorem}
45	
46	
47	
48	\leanok
49	[Generic flatness, algebraic form]
50	  \label{thm:generic_flatness_algebraic}
51	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.genericFlatnessAlgebraic}
52	  % NOTE (iter-022): CLOSED, axiom-clean {propext, Classical.choice, Quot.sound}.
53	  % The Lean decl is single-universe `(A B M : Type u)` (was `Type*`): L5 and
54	  % `free_localizationAway_of_away_tower` are single-universe, and the dévissage
55	  % applies them to `C = B ⧸ 𝔭`, forcing A, B into one universe. Faithful for the
56	  % geometric use (scheme patches are single-universe).
57	  \uses{lem:gf_torsion_base, lem:noeth_prime_filtration, lem:gf_splice_shortExact,
58	        lem:gf_noether_clear_denominators, lem:gf_polynomial_core, lem:gf_finite_module}
59	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4 ``Lemma on Generic Flatness''
60	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1711-L1716).
61	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
62	  % "Let \(A\) be a noetherian domain,
63	  %  and \(B\) a finite type \(A\) algebra. Let \(M\) be a finite \(B\)-module.
64	  %  Then there exists an \(f\in A\), \(f\ne 0\), such that
65	  %  the localisation \(M_f\) is a free module over \(A_f\)."
66	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, ``Lemma on Generic Flatness''.}
67	  Let \(A\) be a noetherian domain, \(B\) a finite-type \(A\)-algebra, and \(M\) a finite
68	  \(B\)-module. Then there exists \(f \in A\), \(f \ne 0\), such that the localisation
69	  \(M_f\) is a free \(A_f\)-module.
70	
71	\end{theorem}
72	
73	% INTENDED LEAN SIGNATURE (the scaffold pass creates the decl pinned above with
74	% exactly this signature):
75	%   (A B M : Type u) [CommRing A] [IsDomain A] [IsNoetherianRing A]   % single-universe (iter-022)
76	%     [CommRing B] [Algebra A B] [Algebra.FiniteType A B]
77	%     [AddCommGroup M] [Module B M] [Module.Finite B M]
78	%     [Module A M] [IsScalarTower A B M] :
79	%     ∃ f : A, f ≠ 0 ∧
80	%       Module.Free (Localization.Away f) (LocalizedModule (Submonoid.powers f) M)
81	
82	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "Over the quotient field \(K\) of \(A\), the \(K\)-algebra
83	% \(B_K = K\otimes _AB\) is of finite type, and \(M_K = K\otimes_AM\) is a
84	% finite module over \(B_K\). Let \(n\) be the dimension of the support
85	% of \(M_K\) over \(\spec B_K\). We argue by induction on \(n\), starting
86	% with \(n=-1\) which is the case when \(M_K =0\). [...]
87	% Now let \(n\ge 0\), and let the lemma be proved for smaller values.
88	% As \(B\) is noetherian and \(M\) is assumed to be a finite \(B\)-module,
89	% there exists a finite filtration
90	% \(0=M_0 \subset \ldots  \subset M_r =M\)
91	% where each \(M_i\) is a \(B\)-submodule of \(M\) such that for each \(i\ge 1\)
92	% the quotient module \(M_i/M_{i-1}\) is isomorphic to \(B/\pp_i\) for
93	% some prime ideal \(\pp_i\) in \(B\). [...] By Noether normalisation lemma,
94	% there exist elements \(b_1,\ldots,b_n \in B\), such that \(K\otimes_AB\) is
95	% finite over its subalgebra \(K[b_1,\ldots, b_n]\) [...]"
96	% (Source: nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1719-L1772; full
97	% proof omitted for length, structural steps restated below in project notation.)
98	\begin{proof}
99	
100	  \textbf{Primary route — the finite \(A\)-module case.}
101	  Suppose first that \(M\) is module-finite over \(A\) itself. Since \(A\) is noetherian,
102	  \(M\) is finitely presented over \(A\). The generic fibre \(M_K = K \otimes_A M\)
103	  (where \(K = \mathrm{Frac}(A)\)) is a finite-dimensional \(K\)-vector space, hence free
104	  over \(K\); by \cref{lem:gf_finite_module} this freeness descends to a single basic open:
105	  there exists \(f \in A\), \(f \ne 0\), with \(M_f\) free over \(A_f\). This closes the
106	  theorem whenever \(M\) is finite over \(A\).
107	
108	  \textbf{Surviving residue — reducing the finite-type \(B\)-algebra case to the
109	  finite \(A\)-module case.} The genuine content not supplied by Mathlib is the
110	  reduction of the general hypothesis (\(M\) finite over a \emph{finite-type}
111	  \(A\)-algebra \(B\)) to the finite \(A\)-module situation handled above. Concretely,
112	  the missing piece is the polynomial-ring core of generic freeness: a finite module
113	  over \(A[b_1, \dots, b_n]\) becomes free over \(A_f\) after inverting a single
114	  \(f \in A\), \(f \ne 0\). The classical \S4 dévissage below is the fallback route
115	  for exactly this residue.
116	
117	  \emph{Fallback (Nitsure \S4 induction).}
118	  The \S4 dévissage is assembled from the sub-lemmas of
119	  \cref{sec:gf_devissage_chain}. If \(M_K = 0\), the torsion base case
120	  \cref{lem:gf_torsion_base} gives \(f \ne 0\) with \(M_f = 0\) free. Otherwise the
121	  prime-filtration induction principle \cref{lem:noeth_prime_filtration} over the
122	  noetherian ring \(B\), combined with the splicing fact
123	  \cref{lem:gf_splice_shortExact}, reduces to \(M = B/\mathfrak{p}\) and hence to
124	  \(B\) a domain, \(M = B\); Noether normalisation with clearing of denominators
125	  \cref{lem:gf_noether_clear_denominators} makes \(B_g\) finite over a polynomial
126	  ring \(A_g[b_1, \dots, b_n]\), and the polynomial-ring core
127	  \cref{lem:gf_polynomial_core} — bottoming out at the finite-\(A\)-module leaf
128	  \cref{lem:gf_finite_module} of the primary route — produces the required
129	  \(f \ne 0\) with \(M_f\) free over \(A_f\).
130	\end{proof}
131	
132	\subsection{Dévissage chain for the finite-type residue}
133	\label{sec:gf_devissage_chain}
134	
135	The following sub-lemmas decompose the surviving residue of
136	\cref{thm:generic_flatness_algebraic} — the case where \(M\) is finite over the
137	finite-type \(A\)-algebra \(B\) but not module-finite over \(A\) — into the
138	classical \cite{nitsure-hilbert-quot}~\S4 induction. They are ordered so that each block
139	depends only on earlier blocks or Mathlib lemmas. Throughout,
140	\(A\) is a noetherian domain with fraction field \(K = \mathrm{Frac}(A)\), \(B\) a
141	finite-type \(A\)-algebra, and \(M\) a finite \(B\)-module with the compatible
142	\(A\)-module structure via the scalar tower \(A \to B \to M\).
143	
144	\begin{lemma}\leanok
145	[Finite-module leaf — finite \(A\)-module case]
146	  \label{lem:gf_finite_module}
147	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.exists_free_localizationAway_of_finite}
148	  \uses{lem:fp_free_descent}
149	  Let \(A\) be a noetherian domain and \(M\) a finite \(A\)-module. Then there
150	  exists \(f \in A\), \(f \ne 0\), such that \(M_f\) is free over \(A_f\).
151	\end{lemma}
152	
153	\begin{proof}
154	
155	  This is the \(d = 0\) leaf of the induction. A finite module over a noetherian ring is
156	  finitely presented, so
157	  \(M\) is finitely presented over \(A\). Over the generic point
158	  \(K = \mathrm{Frac}(A) = S^{-1}A\) (\(S = A \setminus \{0\}\)) the localisation
159	  \(M_K = S^{-1}M\) is a finite-dimensional \(K\)-vector space, hence free over
160	  \(K\). The finite-presentation free-descent lemma \cref{lem:fp_free_descent}
161	  then descends this freeness to a single basic open: there is \(r \in S\), i.e.\
162	  \(r \in A\) with \(r \ne 0\), such that \(M_r\) is free over \(A_r\). Take
163	  \(f := r\).
164	\end{proof}
165	
166	\begin{lemma}[Finite-presentation free descent]
167	  \label{lem:fp_free_descent}
168	  \lean{Module.FinitePresentation.exists_free_localizedModule_powers}
169	  \mathlibok
170	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
171	  Let \(R\) be a commutative ring, \(S \subseteq R\) a multiplicative subset, and
172	  \(M\) a finitely-presented \(R\)-module such that the localisation \(M_S\) is
173	  free over the localisation \(R_S\). Then there exists \(r \in S\) such that
174	  \(M_r\) is free over \(R_r\) (of the same finite rank).
175	\end{lemma}
176	
177	\begin{lemma}\leanok
178	[L1 — torsion base case \(M_K = 0\)]
179	  \label{lem:gf_torsion_base}
180	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.exists_free_localizationAway_of_torsion}
181	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4 ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'', proof, base case
182	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1719-L1726).
183	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
184	  % "We argue by induction on $n$, starting with $n=-1$ which is the case when
185	  %  $M_K =0$. In this case, as $K\otimes _AM = S^{-1}M$ where $S = A - \{ 0\}$,
186	  %  each $v\in M$ is annihilated by some non-zero element of $A$. Taking a finite
187	  %  generating set, and a common multiple of corresponding annihilating elements,
188	  %  we see there exists an $f\ne 0$ in $A$ with $fM=0$. Hence $M_f =0$, proving
189	  %  the lemma when $n=-1$."
190	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'' (base case of the
191	  induction).}
192	  Suppose \(K \otimes_A M = 0\), where \(K = \mathrm{Frac}(A)\). Then there exists
193	  \(f \in A\), \(f \ne 0\), with \(f M = 0\); consequently \(M_f = 0\) is free over
194	  \(A_f\).
195	\end{lemma}
196	
197	\begin{proof}
198	
199	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the comment above the statement (L1719-L1726).
200	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4 (base case).}
201	  Since \(K \otimes_A M = S^{-1}M\) with \(S = A \setminus \{0\}\), the hypothesis
202	  \(M_K = 0\) means every element of \(M\) is killed by some non-zero element of
203	  \(A\). Choose a finite \(B\)-generating set \(v_1, \dots, v_k\) of \(M\) (which
204	  exists because \(M\) is a finite \(B\)-module), and for each \(i\) an
205	  \(a_i \in A\), \(a_i \ne 0\), with \(a_i v_i = 0\). The product
206	  \(f := a_1 \cdots a_k\) is non-zero (\(A\) is a domain) and annihilates every
207	  generator, hence \(f M = 0\). Therefore \(M_f = 0\), which is free over \(A_f\).
208	\end{proof}
209	
210	\begin{lemma}[L2 — prime-filtration induction principle]
211	  \label{lem:noeth_prime_filtration}
212	  \lean{IsNoetherianRing.induction_on_isQuotientEquivQuotientPrime}
213	  \mathlibok
214	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
215	  Let \(B\) be a noetherian commutative ring and let \(P\) be a predicate on finite
216	  \(B\)-modules that (i) holds for every subsingleton module, (ii) holds for every
217	  cyclic quotient \(B/\mathfrak{p}\) with \(\mathfrak{p}\) prime, and (iii) is
218	  closed under short exact sequences (\(P(N_1)\) and \(P(N_3)\) imply \(P(N_2)\)
219	  for \(0 \to N_1 \to N_2 \to N_3 \to 0\)). Then \(P\) holds for every finite
220	  \(B\)-module. In particular every finite \(B\)-module \(M\) admits a finite
221	  filtration \(0 = M_0 \subset \cdots \subset M_r = M\) with each quotient
222	  \(M_i / M_{i-1} \cong B/\mathfrak{p}_i\) for a prime \(\mathfrak{p}_i\), so to
223	  prove generic freeness for \(M\) it suffices to prove it for each
224	  \(B/\mathfrak{p}\) and to splice along the filtration's short exact sequences.
225	\end{lemma}

227	\begin{lemma}\leanok
228	[L3a — localisation of a short exact sequence is exact]
229	  \label{lem:gf_splice_shortExact_localized_exact}
230	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.exact_localizedModule_powers_of_shortExact}
231	  Let \(0 \to M' \xrightarrow{i} M \xrightarrow{q} M'' \to 0\) be a short exact
232	  sequence of \(B\)-modules, and let \(f \in A\), \(f \ne 0\). Restricting scalars
233	  along \(A \to B\) and localising every term at the powers of \(f\) yields a short
234	  exact sequence
235	  \[
236	    0 \to M'_f \xrightarrow{i_f} M_f \xrightarrow{q_f} M''_f \to 0
237	  \]
238	  of \(A_f\)-modules; in particular \(i_f\) is injective and \(q_f\) is surjective.
239	\end{lemma}
240	
241	\begin{proof}
242	
243	  Localisation at a multiplicative set is an exact functor, preserving injections,
244	  surjections, and exactness. Applying it to the scalar-restricted \(A\)-linear maps
245	  \(i\) and \(q\) (via \(A \to B\)) returns the displayed sequence.
246	\end{proof}

248	\begin{lemma}\leanok
249	[L3b — free transport across a finer localisation]
250	  \label{lem:gf_splice_shortExact_free_transport}
251	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.free_localizationAway_of_free_of_eq_mul}
252	  Let \(N\) be a \(B\)-module and \(f', f'' \in A\) with \(f = f' f''\). If the
253	  localisation \(N_{f'}\) is free over \(A_{f'}\), then the further localisation
254	  \(N_f\) is free over \(A_f\).
255	\end{lemma}
256	
257	\begin{proof}
258	
259	  Because \(f = f' f''\), the localisation away from \(f\) factors through the
260	  localisation away from \(f'\): the ring \(A_f\) is the localisation of \(A_{f'}\)
261	  at the image of \(f''\), and likewise the module \(N_f\) is the localisation of
262	  \(N_{f'}\) at the image of \(f''\). A localisation of a free module is free over
263	  the localised base ring — a basis of \(N_{f'}\) over \(A_{f'}\) maps to a basis of
264	  \(N_f\) over \(A_f\). Hence \(N_f\) is free over \(A_f\). Stated as a standalone
265	  fact, it is applied to both ends of the spliced sequence.
266	\end{proof}

268	\begin{lemma}\leanok
269	[L3c — a short exact sequence with free ends has a free middle]
270	  \label{lem:gf_splice_shortExact_split}
271	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.free_of_shortExact_of_free_free}
272	  Let \(0 \to M'_f \to M_f \to M''_f \to 0\) be a short exact sequence of
273	  \(A_f\)-modules in which both ends \(M'_f\) and \(M''_f\) are free over \(A_f\).
274	  Then \(M_f\) is free over \(A_f\); concretely \(M_f \cong M'_f \oplus M''_f\).
275	\end{lemma}
276	
277	\begin{proof}
278	
279	  A free module is projective, so the free quotient \(M''_f\) is projective and the
280	  short exact sequence splits: \(M_f \cong M'_f \oplus M''_f\). A finite direct sum
281	  of free \(A_f\)-modules is free, hence \(M_f\) is free over \(A_f\).
282	\end{proof}

284	\begin{lemma}\leanok
285	[L3 — splicing fact for a short exact sequence (assembly)]
286	  \label{lem:gf_splice_shortExact}
287	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.exists_free_localizationAway_of_shortExact}
288	  \uses{lem:gf_splice_shortExact_localized_exact,
289	        lem:gf_splice_shortExact_free_transport, lem:gf_splice_shortExact_split}
290	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4 ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'', proof, splicing remark
291	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1737-L1745).
292	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
293	  % "Note that if $0\to M'\to M\to M'' \to 0$ is a short exact sequence of
294	  %  $B$-modules, and if $f'$ and $f''$ are non-zero elements of $A$ such that
295	  %  $M'_{f'}$ and $M''_{f''}$ are free over respectively $A_{f'}$ and $A_{f''}$,
296	  %  then $M_f$ is a free module over $A_f$ where $f=f'f''$. We will use this fact
297	  %  repeatedly. Therefore it is enough to prove the result when $M$ is of the form
298	  %  $B/\pp$ for a prime ideal $\pp$ in $B$. This reduces us to the case where $B$
299	  %  is a domain and $M=B$."
300	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'' (splicing fact).}
301	  Let \(0 \to M' \to M \to M'' \to 0\) be a short exact sequence of \(B\)-modules,
302	  and let \(f', f'' \in A\) be non-zero with \(M'_{f'}\) free over \(A_{f'}\) and
303	  \(M''_{f''}\) free over \(A_{f''}\). Then for \(f := f' f''\) the localisation
304	  \(M_f\) is free over \(A_f\).

306	
307	\begin{proof}
308	  \uses{lem:gf_splice_shortExact_localized_exact,
309	        lem:gf_splice_shortExact_free_transport, lem:gf_splice_shortExact_split}
310	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the comment above the statement (L1737-L1745).
311	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4 (splicing fact).}
312	  Set \(f := f' f''\); since \(A\) is a domain and \(f', f'' \ne 0\), the product is
313	  non-zero, so \(f \ne 0\). Localising the given short exact sequence at the powers
314	  of \(f\) (after restricting scalars to \(A\)) produces a short exact sequence
315	  \(0 \to M'_f \to M_f \to M''_f \to 0\) of \(A_f\)-modules, by
316	  \cref{lem:gf_splice_shortExact_localized_exact}. Both ends are free over \(A_f\):
317	  applying \cref{lem:gf_splice_shortExact_free_transport} to \(M'\) with the
318	  factorisation \(f = f' f''\) (using that \(M'_{f'}\) is free over \(A_{f'}\)) shows
319	  \(M'_f\) is free over \(A_f\), and applying the same lemma to \(M''\) with the
320	  factorisation \(f = f'' f'\) (using that \(M''_{f''}\) is free over \(A_{f''}\))
321	  shows \(M''_f\) is free over \(A_f\). Finally
322	  \cref{lem:gf_splice_shortExact_split} concludes that the middle term \(M_f\) is

326	\begin{lemma}[L4 anchor — Noether normalisation over a field]
327	  \label{lem:noether_normalization_fg}
328	  \lean{exists_finite_inj_algHom_of_fg}
329	  \mathlibok

331	  Let \(k\) be a field and \(R\) a non-trivial finite-type \(k\)-algebra. Then there
332	  exist \(s \in \mathbb{N}\) and an injective \(k\)-algebra homomorphism
333	  \(k[X_1, \dots, X_s] \to R\) that is module-finite, i.e.\ \(R\) is a finite module
334	  over a polynomial subalgebra on algebraically independent generators.
335	\end{lemma}

340	\leanok

342	  \label{lem:gf_clear_one_denominator}
343	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.gf_clear_one_denominator}
344	  % NOTE (shared engine): the denominator-clearing performed here -- pushing finitely
345	  % many coefficients of one polynomial from K = Frac(A) down into a single
346	  % Localization.Away g -- is the same primitive the torsion reindex (L5b,
347	  % lem:gf_torsion_reindex) uses when it inverts the leading coefficient of the
348	  % Nagata-normalised annihilator. A prover who writes one general "clear finitely many
349	  % denominators into one Localization.Away g" helper can have both lemmas consume it.
350	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4 ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'', proof, normalisation step
351	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1755-L1759).
352	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
353	  % "If $g\ne 0$ in $A$ is chosen to be a `common denominator' for
354	  %  coefficients of equations of integral dependence
355	  %  satisfied by a finite set of
356	  %  algebra generators for $K\otimes_AB$ over $K[b_1,\ldots, b_n]$,
357	  %  we see that $B_g$ is finite over $A_g[b_1,\ldots, b_n]$."
358	  % LEAN SIGNATURE (the scaffold creates the decl pinned above with this signature).
359	  %   Let K := FractionRing A and A_g := Localization.Away g; the canonical map
360	  %   A_g -> K (induced because powers of g are non-zero-divisors of A) extends along
361	  %   MvPolynomial.map to MvPolynomial (Fin n) A_g -> MvPolynomial (Fin n) K.
362	  %     (A : Type*) [CommRing A] [IsDomain A] (n : ℕ)
363	  %       (p : MvPolynomial (Fin n) (FractionRing A)) :
364	  %       ∃ (g : A) (_ : g ≠ 0),
365	  %         p ∈ Set.range
366	  %           (MvPolynomial.map (algebraMap (Localization.Away g) (FractionRing A)))
367	  % NOTE (encoding): there is NO canonical `Algebra (Localization.Away g)
368	  %   (FractionRing A)` instance, so the landed Lean does not use the literal
369	  %   `algebraMap (Localization.Away g) (FractionRing A)` above. It encodes the same map
370	  %   as `IsLocalization.map (FractionRing A) (RingHom.id A) hle` with
371	  %   `hle : powers g ≤ comap id (nonZeroDivisors A)` — mathematically identical, just the
372	  %   well-typed form. The proof is axiom-clean.
373	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'' (common denominator).}
374	  Let \(A\) be a noetherian domain with fraction field \(K = \mathrm{Frac}(A)\), and let
375	  \(p \in K[X_1, \dots, X_n]\) be a polynomial with coefficients in \(K\) (for instance,
376	  one coefficient polynomial of an equation of integral dependence over
377	  \(K[b_1, \dots, b_n]\)). Then there exists \(g \in A\), \(g \ne 0\), such that \(p\)
378	  lies in the image of the coefficient-extension map
379	  \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_n] \to K[X_1, \dots, X_n]\); equivalently, \(p\) has all of its
380	  coefficients in \(A_g = A[g^{-1}] \subseteq K\).
381	\end{lemma}
382	
383	\begin{proof}
384	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the comment above the statement (L1755-L1759).
385	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4 (common denominator).}
386	  The polynomial \(p\) has finitely many non-zero coefficients
387	  \(c_1, \dots, c_r \in K = \mathrm{Frac}(A)\). Each \(c_j\) is a fraction
388	  \(c_j = a_j / s_j\) with \(a_j \in A\) and \(s_j \in A \setminus \{0\}\). Put
389	  \(g := s_1 \cdots s_r \in A\); since \(A\) is a domain and each \(s_j \ne 0\), also
390	  \(g \ne 0\). For each \(j\) the denominator \(s_j\) divides \(g\), so
391	  \(c_j = a_j / s_j\) lies in the subring \(A_g = A[g^{-1}] \subseteq K\). Hence every
392	  coefficient of \(p\) lies in \(A_g\), and \(p\) is the coefficient-extension of a
393	  polynomial in \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_n]\).
394	\end{proof}

421	  % `algBg : Algebra A_g B_g` is COMPATIBLE with the canonical `A → B → B_g`

427	  % `MvPolynomial (Fin n) A_g`, embedded into B_g by the algebra map φ; the
428	  % generators b_j are φ(X_j). Compared with an earlier draft using three anonymous
429	  % instance existentials (∃ (_ : Algebra A_g B_g) (_ : Algebra (MvPoly … A_g) B_g)
430	  % (_ : IsScalarTower …)), this form keeps only the single `Algebra A_g B_g` binder
431	  % needed to type B_g as an A_g-algebra: the single φ then carries the algebra map,

433	  % `Module.Finite … over φ.toAlgebra` encodes module-finiteness of B_g over the

438	  % inversion g; the integral-dependence equations only clear denominators after
439	  % inverting g (see the SOURCE QUOTE PROOF below).
440	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4 ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'', proof, normalisation step
441	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1747-L1758).
442	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
443	  % "By Noether normalisation lemma, there exist elements $b_1,\ldots,b_n \in B$,
444	  %  such that $K\otimes_AB$ is finite over its subalgebra $K[b_1,\ldots, b_n]$ and
445	  %  the elements $b_1,\ldots, b_n$ are algebraically independent over $K$. [...]
446	  %  If $g\ne 0$ in $A$ is chosen to be a `common denominator' for coefficients of
447	  %  equations of integral dependence satisfied by a finite set of algebra
448	  %  generators for $K\otimes_AB$ over $K[b_1,\ldots, b_n]$, we see that $B_g$ is
449	  %  finite over $A_g[b_1,\ldots, b_n]$."

451	  normalisation with denominators).}
452	  Let \(A\) be a noetherian domain with fraction field \(K\) and \(B\) a finite-type
453	  \(A\)-algebra that is a domain with \(B_K = K \otimes_A B \ne 0\). Write
454	  \(A_g = A[g^{-1}]\) and \(B_g = B[g^{-1}]\) for the localisations away from \(g\)
455	  and its image. Then there exist a natural number \(n\), an element \(g \in A\) with
456	  \(g \ne 0\), and an injective \(A_g\)-algebra homomorphism
457	  \[
458	    \varphi : A_g[X_1, \dots, X_n] \longrightarrow B_g,
459	  \]
460	  where \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_n]\) is the polynomial ring on \(n\) algebraically
461	  independent variables, such that \(B_g\) is module-finite over the image polynomial
462	  subalgebra via \(\varphi\). The injectivity of \(\varphi\) records the algebraic
463	  independence of the generators \(b_j := \varphi(X_j)\), and module-finiteness is
464	  taken over the algebra structure that \(\varphi\) puts on \(B_g\).

466	
467	\begin{proof}
468	  \uses{lem:noether_normalization_fg, lem:gf_clear_one_denominator}
469	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the comment above the statement (L1747-L1758).
470	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4 (Noether normalisation with denominators).}
471	  % NOTE: the denominator-clearing middle of this proof is a Finset-fold over the finite
472	  % generating set sigma, but each fold step is the Mathlib collapsing lemma
473	  % IsIntegral.exists_multiple_integral_of_isLocalization applied to the generator AS A
474	  % WHOLE (not to its individual coefficient polynomials via gf_clear_one_denominator).
475	  % For each generator x, that lemma yields a single a_x in A^0 (nonzero, since A is a
476	  % domain) with C(a_x) * (1 (x) x) integral over MvPolynomial A; set g1 := prod_x a_x and
477	  % g := g0 * g1. The AlgHom assembly below then runs over A_g exactly as before.
478	
479	  \emph{Step 1 — Noether normalisation over \(K\).} Apply Noether normalisation over the
480	  field \(K\) (\cref{lem:noether_normalization_fg}) to the finite-type \(K\)-algebra
481	  \(B_K = K \otimes_A B\): there are algebraically independent
482	  \(\bar b_1, \dots, \bar b_n \in B_K\) with \(B_K\) module-finite over
483	  \(K[\bar b_1, \dots, \bar b_n]\). Clearing denominators, each \(\bar b_j\) is
484	  represented by some \(b_j \in B\) (writing \(1 \otimes b\) as \(b\)).
485	
486	  \emph{Step 2 — clear the integral-dependence denominators to a single \(g\).} Fix a
487	  finite set \(\sigma\) of algebra generators of \(B_K\) over
488	  \(K[\bar b_1, \dots, \bar b_n]\). By Step 1 each generator's image
489	  \(1 \otimes x \in B_K\) is integral over \(K[\bar b_1, \dots, \bar b_n]\). For each
490	  \(x \in \sigma\) apply the Mathlib collapsing lemma
491	  \texttt{IsIntegral.exists\_multiple\_integral\_of\_isLocalization} (with
492	  \(R = \mathrm{MvPoly}\,A\), \(R_m = \mathrm{MvPoly}\,K\), \(S = B_K\), and the localizing
493	  submonoid the image of \(A^{0}\)): this returns a single
494	  \(a_x \in A^{0}\) (non-zero, as \(A\) is a domain) such that \(C(a_x)\cdot(1\otimes x)\)
495	  is integral over \(\mathrm{MvPoly}\,A = A[b_1, \dots, b_n]\). Multiplying over the finite
496	  set, set \(g_1 := \prod_{x \in \sigma} a_x\) and \(g := g_0 \cdot g_1\) (with \(g_0\) the
497	  Step-1 denominator witness). After inverting \(g\), every chosen generator becomes
498	  integral over \(A_g[b_1, \dots, b_n]\) and the generators generate \(B_g\) as an algebra;
499	  hence \(B_g\) is module-finite over \(A_g[b_1, \dots, b_n]\). (The single-coefficient
500	  lemma \cref{lem:gf_clear_one_denominator} provides the same denominator-clearing primitive
501	  at the level of one coefficient polynomial; the landed proof discharges a whole generator
502	  at once via the collapsing lemma, which folds that primitive over the coefficients
503	  internally.)
504	
505	  \emph{Step 3 — AlgHom assembly.} The algebra homomorphism \(\varphi\) of the statement
506	  is the \(A_g\)-algebra map \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_n] \to B_g\) sending \(X_j \mapsto b_j\),
507	  where \(b_j \in B_g\) is the image of \(b_j \in B\). We establish injectivity and
508	  module-finiteness through a comparison with the situation over \(K\).
509	
510	  \emph{Step 3a — the comparison map \(\nu : B_g \to B_K\).} Since \(g \ne 0\) in the
511	  domain \(A\) and \(A \subseteq K\), the image of \(g\) is a unit of the field \(K\), so
512	  it is invertible in \(B_K = K \otimes_A B\). Hence the canonical \(A\)-algebra map
513	  \(B \to B_K\), \(b \mapsto 1 \otimes b\), sends \(g\) to a unit and, by the universal
514	  property of localisation, factors through a canonical \(A\)-algebra map
515	  \[
516	    \nu : B_g = B[g^{-1}] \longrightarrow B_K, \qquad \nu(b/g^k) = (1 \otimes b)\,(1 \otimes g)^{-k}.
517	  \]
518	  The map \(\nu\) is injective. Indeed, \(B\) is a domain, and both \(B_g\) and \(B_K\) are
519	  localisations of \(B\) at multiplicative subsets of the non-zerodivisors: \(B_g\) inverts
520	  the powers of \(g\), while \(B_K = K \otimes_A B\) inverts (in particular) the images of
521	  all non-zero elements of \(A\), a multiplicative set containing the powers of \(g\). A
522	  localisation of a domain at a set of non-zerodivisors is injective into any further such
523	  localisation. Concretely, the natural map \(B \to B_K\) is injective on the domain \(B\)
524	  (its kernel is the set of \(b\) annihilated by some non-zero element of \(A\), which in a
525	  domain over which \(A\) acts faithfully is \(0\)), so \(\nu(b/g^k) = 0\) forces
526	  \(1 \otimes b = 0\), hence \(b = 0\) and \(b/g^k = 0\). Under \(\nu\) the lifts
527	  \(b_j \in B_g\) of the Noether generators map to the elements \(\bar b_j \in B_K\) of
528	  Step 1: \(\nu(b_j) = \bar b_j\).
529	
530	  \emph{Step 3b — injectivity of \(\varphi\) by algebraic-independence descent
531	  \(K \to A_g\).} Injectivity of the \(A_g\)-algebra map
532	  \(\varphi : A_g[X_1, \dots, X_n] \to B_g\), \(X_j \mapsto b_j\), is equivalent to the
533	  algebraic independence of the family \((b_j)\) over \(A_g\): a non-zero element of the
534	  kernel is exactly a non-trivial \(A_g\)-polynomial relation \(P(b_1, \dots, b_n) = 0\).
535	  The localisation \(A_g = A[g^{-1}] \subseteq K\) embeds in the fraction field, giving an
536	  injection of \(A_g\)-algebras \(A_g \hookrightarrow K\). Now suppose
537	  \(P \in A_g[X_1, \dots, X_n]\) is non-zero with \(P(b_1, \dots, b_n) = 0\) in \(B_g\).
538	  Applying the injective map \(\nu\) and using \(\nu(b_j) = \bar b_j\) together with the fact
539	  that \(\nu\) is an \(A\)-algebra map extending scalars to \(K\), we obtain
540	  \(P^K(\bar b_1, \dots, \bar b_n) = 0\) in \(B_K\), where \(P^K\) is the image of \(P\)
541	  under the coefficient inclusion \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_n] \hookrightarrow K[X_1, \dots, X_n]\).
542	  Since \(A_g \hookrightarrow K\) is injective, \(P \ne 0\) gives \(P^K \ne 0\); but the
543	  \(\bar b_j\) are algebraically independent over \(K\) (Step 1), so the only \(K\)-polynomial
544	  vanishing on them is \(0\), a contradiction. Hence no non-trivial relation exists, \((b_j)\)
545	  is algebraically independent over \(A_g\), and \(\varphi\) is injective.
546	
547	  \emph{Step 3c — module-finiteness of \(B_g\) over \(\operatorname{im}\varphi\).} By Step 1,
548	  \(B_K\) is module-finite over \(K[\bar b_1, \dots, \bar b_n]\); fix a finite set of algebra
549	  generators of \(B_K\) over \(K[\bar b_1, \dots, \bar b_n]\) (Step 2). Each such generator
550	  satisfies a monic equation of integral dependence whose coefficients are polynomials in
551	  \(K[\bar b_1, \dots, \bar b_n]\). The element \(g\) of Step 2 was chosen as a common
552	  denominator for the (finitely many) coefficient polynomials of these equations, so that
553	  after inverting \(g\) every such coefficient already lies in
554	  \(A_g[b_1, \dots, b_n] = \operatorname{im}\varphi\). Each chosen generator is the
555	  \(\nu\)-image of an element of \(B_g\), and its integral-dependence equation is the
556	  \(\nu\)-image of the corresponding monic equation holding in \(B_g\) with coefficients in
557	  \(\operatorname{im}\varphi\); since \(\nu\) is injective, that equation holds in \(B_g\)
558	  itself. Thus each generator of \(B_g\) is integral over the subalgebra
559	  \(\operatorname{im}\varphi\), and these generators generate \(B_g\) as an
560	  \(\operatorname{im}\varphi\)-algebra. A finite-type algebra that is integral over a
561	  subalgebra is module-finite over it; hence \(B_g\) is module-finite over
562	  \(\operatorname{im}\varphi = A_g[b_1, \dots, b_n]\). Transported along the injection
563	  \(\varphi\), this is exactly module-finiteness of \(B_g\) over \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_n]\) for
564	  the algebra structure that \(\varphi\) puts on \(B_g\), as claimed. This recovers the
565	  source's conclusion that \(B_g\) is finite over \(A_g[b_1, \dots, b_n]\).
566	\end{proof}
567	
568	\begin{lemma}\leanok
569	[injectivity of a localisation lift]
570	  \label{lem:gf_isLocalization_lift_injective}
571	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.isLocalization_lift_injective}
572	  Let \(S\) be a localisation of a ring \(R\) at a multiplicative set \(M\), and let
573	  \(g : R \to P\) be a ring homomorphism sending every element of \(M\) to a unit of
574	  \(P\), so that it induces the lift \(\widehat g : S \to P\). If both the localisation
575	  map \(R \to S\) and \(g\) itself are injective, then \(\widehat g\) is injective.
576	\end{lemma}
577	\begin{proof}
578	  The lift is characterised by \(\widehat g(r/m) = g(r)\,g(m)^{-1}\). By the universal
579	  criterion for injectivity of a localisation lift, \(\widehat g\) is injective iff for
580	  all \(r_1, r_2 \in R\) the equality of localisation images
581	  \((r_1 \mapsto S) = (r_2 \mapsto S)\) implies \(g(r_1) = g(r_2)\). Both the hypothesis
582	  and the conclusion of that implication reduce, via injectivity of \(R \to S\) and of
583	  \(g\), to the single condition \(r_1 = r_2\); hence the implication holds and
584	  \(\widehat g\) is injective. (This helper is applied twice in
585	  \cref{lem:gf_noether_clear_denominators}, to the comparison maps \(\nu : B_g \to B_K\)
586	  and \(\psi : A_g \to K\).)
587	\end{proof}
588	
589	\begin{lemma}
590	
591	
592	\leanok
593	[L5a — the generic-rank short exact sequence]
594	  \label{lem:gf_generic_rank_ses}
595	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.gf_generic_rank_ses}
596	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4 ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'', proof, inductive step
597	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1760-L1765).
598	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
599	  % "Let $m$ be the generic rank of the finite module $B_g$ over the
600	  %  domain $A_g[b_1,\ldots, b_n]$. Then we have a short exact
601	  %  sequence of $A_g[b_1,\ldots, b_n]$-modules of the form
602	  %  $$0\to A_g[b_1,\ldots, b_n]^{\oplus m} \to B_g \to T \to 0$$
603	  %  where $T$ is a finite torsion module over $A_g[b_1,\ldots, b_n]$."
604	  % LEAN SIGNATURE (the scaffold creates the decl pinned above with this signature).
605	  %   Write P_d := MvPolynomial (Fin d) A.
606	  %     (A : Type*) [CommRing A] [IsDomain A] [IsNoetherianRing A]
607	  %       (d : ℕ) (N : Type*) [AddCommGroup N]
608	  %       [Module (MvPolynomial (Fin d) A) N]
609	  %       [Module.Finite (MvPolynomial (Fin d) A) N]
610	  %       [Module A N] [IsScalarTower A (MvPolynomial (Fin d) A) N] :
611	  %       ∃ (m : ℕ) (φ : (Fin m → MvPolynomial (Fin d) A)
612	  %                        →ₗ[MvPolynomial (Fin d) A] N),
613	  %         Function.Injective φ ∧
614	  %         Module.IsTorsion (MvPolynomial (Fin d) A) (N ⧸ LinearMap.range φ)
615	  %   witness: m = Module.finrank (FractionRing P_d)
616	  %                  (LocalizedModule (nonZeroDivisors P_d) N), the generic rank;
617	  %            φ = Fintype.linearCombination P_d v, with v : Fin m → N the
618	  %            denominator-cleared lifts of a FractionRing P_d-basis of the localisation.
619	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'' (generic-rank SES).}
620	  Let \(A\) be a noetherian domain, \(d \ge 0\), and \(N\) a finite module over the
621	  polynomial ring \(P_d := A[X_1, \dots, X_d]\) (with its induced \(A\)-module structure
622	  through the scalar tower \(A \to P_d \to N\)). Then there exist \(m \in \mathbb{N}\) and
623	  an injective \(P_d\)-linear map \(\varphi : P_d^{\oplus m} \to N\) whose cokernel
624	  \(T := N / \operatorname{im}\varphi\) is a torsion \(P_d\)-module. Equivalently, there is
625	  a short exact sequence of \(P_d\)-modules
626	  \[
627	    0 \to P_d^{\oplus m} \xrightarrow{\ \varphi\ } N \to T \to 0,
628	    \qquad T \text{ torsion}.
629	  \]
630	
631	  The integer \(m\) is the \emph{generic rank} of \(N\): the dimension of the
632	  \(\mathrm{Frac}(P_d)\)-vector space
633	  \(\mathrm{Frac}(P_d) \otimes_{P_d} N\), i.e.\ of the localisation of \(N\) at the
634	  non-zero-divisors of the domain \(P_d\). This step lives over \(P_d\) directly —
635	  \emph{no inversion of any element \(g \in A\) is required here}; the element \(g\) of
636	  Nitsure's argument enters only at the reindexing step \cref{lem:gf_torsion_reindex}.
637	\end{lemma}
638	
639	\begin{proof}
640	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the comment above the statement (L1760-L1765).
641	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4 (generic-rank SES).}
642	  Write \(P_d = A[X_1, \dots, X_d]\); since \(A\) is a noetherian domain, so is \(P_d\)
643	  (Hilbert basis theorem, and a polynomial ring over a domain is a domain). Let
644	  \(Q = \mathrm{Frac}(P_d)\) be its fraction field and
645	  \(N_Q := Q \otimes_{P_d} N\) the localisation of \(N\) at the non-zero-divisors of
646	  \(P_d\); as \(N\) is finite over \(P_d\), \(N_Q\) is a finite-dimensional
647	  \(Q\)-vector space. Put \(m := \dim_Q N_Q\), the generic rank.
648	
649	  Choose a \(Q\)-basis of \(N_Q\) and clear denominators: each basis vector is a
650	  \(Q\)-unit multiple of the image of some \(v_i \in N\), so the images of
651	  \(v_1, \dots, v_m \in N\) again form a \(Q\)-basis of \(N_Q\). In particular the family
652	  \((v_i)_{i}\) is linearly independent over \(Q\); restricting scalars along the
653	  injection \(P_d \hookrightarrow Q\), it is linearly independent over \(P_d\). Let
654	  \(\varphi : P_d^{\oplus m} \to N\) be the \(P_d\)-linear map
655	  \((c_1, \dots, c_m) \mapsto \sum_i c_i v_i\). Linear independence of \((v_i)\) over
656	  \(P_d\) makes \(\varphi\) injective.
657	
658	  Set \(T := N / \operatorname{im}\varphi\). Because the images of \(v_1, \dots, v_m\)
659	  already span \(N_Q\) over \(Q\), the localised map \(\varphi \otimes Q : Q^{\oplus m}
660	  \to N_Q\) is an isomorphism, so \(Q \otimes_{P_d} T = 0\). A finite \(P_d\)-module whose
661	  localisation at the non-zero-divisors of \(P_d\) vanishes is torsion; hence \(T\) is a
662	  torsion \(P_d\)-module. This is the asserted short exact sequence.
663	\end{proof}

675	\begin{lemma}[L5b anchor — a finite torsion module has a non-zero-divisor annihilator]
676	  \label{lem:annihilator_meets_nonZeroDivisors}
677	  \lean{Submodule.annihilator_top_inter_nonZeroDivisors}
678	  \mathlibok
679	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4 ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'', proof, base case $n=-1$
680	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1724-L1727)
681	  % -- the same torsion-annihilation primitive, here provided in general by Mathlib.
682	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
683	  % "each $v\in M$ is annihilated by some non-zero element of $A$. Taking a finite
684	  %  generating set, and a common multiple of corresponding annihilating
685	  %  elements, we see there exists an $f\ne 0$ in $A$ with $fM=0$."
686	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
687	  Let \(R\) be a commutative ring and \(M\) a finite torsion \(R\)-module. Then the
688	  annihilator of \(M\) meets the non-zero-divisors of \(R\): the set
689	  \((\top : \operatorname{Submodule} R\,M).\mathrm{annihilator} \cap R^{\circ}\) is
690	  non-empty, where \(R^{\circ}\) denotes the non-zero-divisors of \(R\).
691	\end{lemma}
692	
693	\begin{lemma}\leanok
694	[L5b.1 — annihilator extraction for the torsion module]
695	  \label{lem:gf_torsion_annihilator}
696	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.gf_torsion_annihilator}
697	  \uses{lem:annihilator_meets_nonZeroDivisors}
698	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4 ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'', proof, base case $n=-1$
699	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1724-L1727).
700	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
701	  % "each $v\in M$ is annihilated by some non-zero element of $A$. Taking a finite
702	  %  generating set, and a common multiple of corresponding annihilating
703	  %  elements, we see there exists an $f\ne 0$ in $A$ with $fM=0$."
704	  % LEAN SIGNATURE (the scaffold creates the decl pinned above with this signature).
705	  %   Write P_d := MvPolynomial (Fin d) A. The conclusion is the existence of a
706	  %   non-zero annihilator; the downstream consequence "T is finite over P_d/(F)" is
707	  %   left to the assembly (a surjection P_d ↠ P_d/(F) pushes a finite generating set).
708	  %     (A : Type*) [CommRing A] [IsDomain A] [IsNoetherianRing A] (d : ℕ)
709	  %       (T : Type*) [AddCommGroup T] [Module (MvPolynomial (Fin d) A) T]
710	  %       [Module.Finite (MvPolynomial (Fin d) A) T]
711	  %       (htors : Module.IsTorsion (MvPolynomial (Fin d) A) T) :
712	  %       ∃ F : MvPolynomial (Fin d) A, F ≠ 0 ∧
713	  %         F ∈ Module.annihilator (MvPolynomial (Fin d) A) T
714	  %   (F ∈ annihilator unfolds to ∀ x : T, F • x = 0.)
715	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'' (torsion annihilation).}
716	  Let \(A\) be a noetherian domain, \(d \ge 0\), and \(T\) a finite \emph{torsion} module
717	  over \(P_d := A[X_1, \dots, X_d]\). Then there exists a non-zero
718	  \(F \in \operatorname{Ann}_{P_d}(T)\); consequently the \(P_d\)-action on \(T\) factors
719	  through \(P_d/(F)\) and \(T\) is a finite module over \(P_d/(F)\).
720	\end{lemma}
721	
722	\begin{proof}
723	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the comment above the statement (L1724-L1727).
724	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4 (torsion annihilation).}
725	  The ring \(P_d = A[X_1, \dots, X_d]\) is a noetherian domain (a polynomial ring over the
726	  noetherian domain \(A\)), and \(T\) is a finite torsion \(P_d\)-module. By
727	  \cref{lem:annihilator_meets_nonZeroDivisors} the annihilator of \(T\) meets the
728	  non-zero-divisors of \(P_d\); choose \(F\) in that intersection. In the domain \(P_d\) a
729	  non-zero-divisor is exactly a non-zero element, so \(F \ne 0\), and \(F \cdot T = 0\).
730	  Since \(F\) annihilates \(T\), the \(P_d\)-module structure descends along the quotient
731	  map \(P_d \twoheadrightarrow P_d/(F)\); the image of any finite \(P_d\)-generating set of
732	  \(T\) generates \(T\) over \(P_d/(F)\), so \(T\) is finite over \(P_d/(F)\).
733	\end{proof}
